## Tool Result: call_qD87SKSe6HAYf5MPJH2CzNr2
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T16:31:23.668Z

[{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.3 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.3 seconds\nOutput:\n# Architecture\r\n\r\n## Phase 3 cinematic companion projection\r\n\r\nThe player reads one server-filtered `PublicSnapshot` containing chapters, released hints/annotations, visible map locations/routes, safe artifact states, visible optional mysteries, event-derived log entries, generic finale state, and per-section unseen counts. One SSE connection transports ordered sanitized events; section navigation is client-side and starts no independent polling.\r\n\r\nNew normalized records are `MapRoute` and `ViewedContent`. Existing content models gained release, relationship, placement, and safe-label fields rather than duplicating derived display tables. `AudioPreference` now carries the cross-device preference projection; immediate UI choices may also be cached locally for offline startup.\r\n\r\n```mermaid\r\nflowchart LR\r\n  P[Player journal + animation director] -->|snapshot + access cookie| N[Next.js server]\r\n  G[Quartermaster] -->|session + CSRF| N\r\n  N -->|Prisma transaction| D[(SQLite dev / MySQL prod)]\r\n  N -->|ordered SSE + replay| P\r\n  N --> A[Audit and save-state log]\r\n```\r\n\r\nServer Components enforce initial access. Route handlers own authentication, validation, snapshots, and SSE. `src/server/progression.ts` is the transaction boundary; `src/domain/story.ts` owns state rules. The UI receives only a public projection, never database rows. In-memory publish accelerates same-process delivery while database replay by sequence remains authoritative after reconnect/restart.\r\n\r\nThe client adds one animation director between ordered domain events and presentation. It starts server work immediately, allows only non-authoritative opening/idle motion while pending, and selects a success or failure timeline from the actual response. One queue owns SSE ceremonies, and the public snapshot remains authoritative after skip, cancellation, reconnect, or visual-runtime failure.\r\n\r\nMajor dependencies remain deliberately bounded: Prisma for persistence and transactions, bcryptjs for portable hashes, Zod for payload validation, GSAP for cinematic orchestration, Motion for React interaction/presence, StPageFlip for the journal surface, Rive/Lottie for isolated vector assets, Pino for structured redacted logs, and Vitest/Playwright for validation. See `docs/animation/architecture.md` and `docs/animation/library-ownership.md`.\r\n\r\nPhase 3 adds an administrative intent layer. `/api/gm/preview` projects without writes, `/api/gm/staging` persists unreleased intent, and `/api/gm/commands` enforces expected sequence and idempotency. `PlayerPresence` supplies expiring evidence. See [admin command pipeline](admin-command-pipeline.md) and [player presence](player-presence-and-sync.md).\r\n\r\n## Tall Tale Studio Phase 1\r\n\r\nStudio is additive to the original campaign companion. `TallTale` owns identity and catalog metadata; `TaleDraft` owns optimistic autosave state; chapters, blocks, and connections form the editable graph. Publishing validates that graph and writes a complete, checksummed `PublishedTaleVersion.contentSnapshot`. Published rows and referenced asset variants are immutable from the player runtime's perspective.\r\n\r\n`src/tall-tale/progression.ts` is the authoritative session engine. Each non-preview `TaleSession` is pinned to one published version and advances through idempotent, ordered events. The player receives its current projection and SSE updates; Captain actions call the same engine. Verification providers all enter through one versioned submission contract, so the simulator and a future paired helper cannot bypass progression rules. See `docs/tall-tale-studio.md`.\r\n\r\n## Unified Tall Tale Platform\r\n\r\nThe platform extends the Studio/session model rather than copying it. `PlayerProfile`/`PlayerIdentitySession` add durable Player identity; `PlaythroughMembership` is the per-Player authorization and library record; `Invitation`/`InvitationEvent` own single-recipient credential lifecycle; `RevealState` is the historical disclosure ledger; `PlatformRoleAssignment` supplements staff capability scope; and `PlatformAuditEvent` records correlated resource actions. `TaleSession` remains the one playthrough aggregate and retains the exact immutable version relation.\r\n\r\n`src/platform/auth.ts`, `policy.ts`, `state.ts`, `invitations.ts`, `libraries.ts`, and `audit.ts` separate authentication, policy, lifecycle, transactional invitation behavior, workspace-specific projections, and safe auditing. Player, Captain, and Creator route families call those services and never serialize a raw aggregate. Compatibility routes call the same progression engine. See [Tall Tale Platform](tall-tale-platform.md) for lifecycle, migration, and security detail.\r\n\r\n## Canonical Player journal\r\n\r\nThe Player Library is the place where Tall Tales are browsed. The immersive journal is the place where Tall Tales are played. `/player/playthroughs/[id]/journal` is therefore the canonical active, resumed, paused, and completed Player route; compatibility play and archive routes converge on the same component.\r\n\r\n`src/tall-tale/progression.ts` remains authoritative. It projects a recursive Player-safe `journal` view from the pinned published snapshot, reveal ledger, ordered events, and current session pointers. `src/tall-tale/journal-contract.ts` defines the ten presentation modes and secret boundary, while `src/tall-tale/journal-page-model.ts` converts only released content into stable physical pages. `PhysicalJournalBook` owns the shared book shell and `TallTaleJournalSession` owns SSE reconciliation, reading position, presentation state, and Player actions. Reading state is persisted separately from progression through the existing Player preferences record.\r\n\r\nThis separation keeps Creator content canonical, the Captain engine authoritative, and historical reading immutable. A future location/vision provider can submit a scoped verification outcome through the existing envelope; the resulting ordered event and canonical state projection drive the same journal without a provider-specific Player runtime.\r\n# Design system\r\n\r\n## Product application foundation\r\n\r\nThe ocean-dark, parchment, brass, moonlight, and muted-crimson materials now serve both the immersive story runtime and the surrounding product. `src/styles/tokens.css` defines semantic background, surface, text, border, accent, state, radius, control-height, content-width, shadow, motion, font, shell, and layer tokens. Product surfaces should use those semantic tokens before adding route-local values. Primary, secondary, subtle, destructive, icon, disabled, loading, and focus states share one control language; disabled controls must remain readable on both ocean and parchment surfaces.\r\n\r\n`ProductShell` is the shared application frame for public discovery and Player, Captain, and Creator workspaces. It owns the skip link, route-aware product mark, active navigation, keyboard-safe mobile menu, workspace language, and restrained footer. Immersive routesâ€”including the role gateway, legacy live companion, platform journal, active Tale runtime, Quartermaster, and development showcaseâ€”deliberately remain outside that chrome so story play is not visually demoted.\r\n\r\n`AsyncState` supplies stable loading, recoverable error, intentional empty, and status-banner patterns. Loading reserves meaningful space and names the work in progress; error states say what failed and offer an in-place retry or a safe return; empty states explain what can happen next; successful and destructive mutations use polite status or alert regions. Route-family layouts provide meaningful document titles while the root metadata remains reusable product copy.\r\n\r\nSystem copy uses the shared product vocabulary: a Creator authors a reusable **Tall Tale**, a Captain configures a version-pinned **Voyage**, an invited **Player** waits for launch, and the **Journal** is the canonical in-session and completed-history surface. The collective word _participant_ is used when a role distinction is unnecessary; _crew_ remains optional flavor rather than permission language. Personal names and event-specific language belong to authored content and invitations, not generic product controls.\r\n\r\n## Cinematic companion sections\r\n\r\nThe shared ocean-dark, parchment, brass, moonlight, and muted-crimson tokens remain authoritative. Journal uses ink/page hierarchy; Chart uses fog and cartographic marks; Altar uses wood/brass placement; Ledger uses secret-note rhythm; Log uses an engraved timeline; Finale uses a dormant celestial mechanism. All retain the same focus ring, corner language, shadows, texture ceiling, typography scale, and motion tokens.\r\n\r\nTokens live in `src/styles/tokens.css`: abyss navy `#061316`, ocean green `#123b3a`, parchment `#d9c69a`, antique brass `#b89551`, crimson `#782f35`, and moonlit teal `#79d2c3`. Georgia/system serif keeps story text readable; Arial is reserved for small operational labels. Paper grain, edge wear, chart lines, brass frames, restrained glow, and vignette are original CSS/SVG materials. Focus is a 3px warm-gold outline. Layers reserve 20â€“35 for persistent chrome, 60 for journal opening, 70 for ceremony controls, and 90 for GM confirmations.\r\n\r\n`globals.css` imports logical material modules: tokens, shell, landing, platform, Tall Tale, Studio, player, GM, animation runtime, and development showcase. Physical section components own their nearby scene targets. Each GSAP target uses a dedicated inner element so Motion can own the interactive outer wrapper without property conflicts.\r\n\r\nMotion is semantic: full, gentle, and reduced retain the same content and order. Reduced mode removes page curl, travel, parallax, particles, and ambient loops but keeps clear state changes. Typography remains one coherent DOM copy during SplitText animation; the split wrappers are reverted after the scene.\r\n# Journal system\r\n\r\n## Governing Player rule\r\n\r\n**The Player Library is the place where Tall Tales are browsed. The immersive journal is the place where Tall Tales are played.** Cards remain selectors in `/player/library`; they do not become the live story surface.\r\n\r\nPublished playthroughs use one physical journal renderer at `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal`. The legacy token route renders that same component, and the old archive route redirects to it. Active, paused, resumed, and completed playthroughs therefore share one visual and interaction model. The original campaign journal and the published Tall Tale journal also share `PhysicalJournalBook` and `PageFlipBook`, preserving the cover, attached latch, seal, binding, left/right page geometry, page stacks, page curl, reduced-motion reader, and responsive transformations.\r\n\r\n`journal-contract.ts` is the Player presentation boundary. Its typed modes are story, riddle, map, artifact, decision, objective, location verification, message, cinematic, and chapter complete. A small optional presentation object selects spread mode, page-turn behavior, and paper/ink treatment without duplicating story content. The recursive Player projection removes answer keys, solutions, Captain/Creator notes, condition expressions, future branches, private consequences, and unreleased hints before the renderer sees them. Logic-only condition and variable blocks remain in canonical progression but do not become readable leaves.\r\n\r\n`journal-page-model.ts` builds stable title, edition, chapter, block, and endpaper pages from the released projection. Current progress remains in the version-pinned `TaleSession`, `RevealState`, inventory, verification records, and ordered events. Per-Player reading position, drawer, text scale, and presentation preferences are stored separately in `PlayerProfile.preferences`; moving through older pages never moves the story. Completed journals use the same released pages from the pinned immutable edition, disable mutations, and retain the checksum/edition record.\r\n\r\nThe existing authenticated SSE endpoint is the single live transport. Reconnect resumes after the last processed sequence, duplicate or stale events only refresh canonical state, and a newly released block turns the physical journal only while the reader is following live progress. Otherwise the journal announces new content and exposes Return to Current Objective. Captain-driven changes and helper verification enter through the progression engine, never through a second journal state machine.\r\n\r\nThe book is dominant on desktop, becomes a compact two-page layout at medium widths, and becomes one readable parchment sheet with bottom drawers and a persistent objective control on narrow or short-landscape screens. Page-turn controls, chapter tabs, drawers, actions, text scaling, Escape, Arrow/Page keys, live announcements, focus-visible states, and reduced-motion behavior remain keyboard and assistive-technology accessible.\r\n\r\n## Original campaign journal\r\n\r\nChapters use `LOCKED`, `TEASER`, `READY`, `REVEALING`, `ACTIVE`, `SOLVED`, and `COMPLETE`. Locked/ready chapters serialize only ordinal, state, and an explicitly safe teaser. Narrative, objective, clue, released hints, annotations, and cross-links serialize only for readable states.\r\n\r\nHints are ordered records with a nullable release time. The server omits unreleased hints. Viewed state uses the player access identity and stable content keys, so it reconciles across devices that share the invitation identity. Annotations are released `JournalEntry` records and never include GM audit metadata.\r\n\r\n`buildJournalPages` converts only the sanitized public snapshot into stable physical page IDs: hard covers, endpapers, title/dedication, chapter dividers, readable narrative/objective/riddle leaves, sealed locked leaves, and back matter. StPageFlip receives selectable semantic HTML, updates its existing instance with `updateFromHtml`, preserves safe current indices, emits orientation/page events, and is destroyed on unmount. Keyboard buttons and Arrow/Page keys turn pages. Reduced motion renders the same page model without page curl.\r\n\r\nTo add a page type, extend the discriminated page model and its secret-filtering/index-stability tests, render one semantic copy in `JournalWorkspace`, and keep its ID based on stable public keys. See `docs/animation/architecture.md`.\r\n# Testing\r\n\r\nRun the complete release gate with:\r\n\r\n```powershell\r\nnpm run validate\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Complete validation gate\r\n\r\nThe command creates a clean local runtime and disposable database, then runs Prisma generation/migration/seed, database invariants, Prettier, ESLint, TypeScript, the complete Vitest suite, the Chromium/WebKit browser matrix, post-acceptance database checks, production build, and two production restart proofs.\r\n\r\nThe live workflow covers player/GM access, rate limiting, cookie isolation, prepare without disclosure, SSE release, ceremony timing/replay/preferences, persistence, duplicate rejection, award/map/solve/undo, offline pause/resume reconciliation, and heartbeat. Command Center tests add preview nonmutation, stale conflict, idempotent replay, all authenticated workspaces, mobile emergency layout, axe accessibility, and visual captures across desktop/tablet/mobile.\r\n\r\nArtifacts are ignored and written below the validation runtime at `artifacts/validation`; Command Center screenshots are in `artifacts/validation/command-center`. `PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL` and `FOREVER_VALIDATION_PRODUCTION_PORT` may select isolated ports for parallel sessions.\r\n\r\n## Integrated validation workflow\r\n\r\nThe command mirrors network-share checkouts into a clean local runtime, rebuilds disposable `validation.db`, and runs:\r\n\r\n1. Exact lockfile install, Prisma generation, migration deployment, seed, and baseline database invariants.\r\n2. Prettier, ESLint, strict TypeScript, Vitest component/unit tests, and local animation-asset validation.\r\n3. Seed verification, pinned Chromium/WebKit installation, and Playwright acceptance/accessibility workflows.\r\n4. Post-acceptance event/snapshot/audit and undo-state verification.\r\n5. Optimized Next.js production build.\r\n6. Two consecutive production start/health/stop cycles, including proof that `/dev/animations` returns 404.\r\n\r\nAnimation coverage includes director queue/synchronization/skip/failure/cancellation/cleanup, every scene builder in all modes, semantic labels, ownership, visibility observers, asset contracts, local Lottie lifecycle/controls/failure, local Rive inputs/lifecycle/WebGL fallback, StPageFlip initialization/update/destroy/keyboard/orientation/reduced behavior, journal secret filtering/stable pages, Motion variants/navigation/objective controls, modal focus restoration, first-arrival replay/skip, and the guarded showcase/trailer.\r\n\r\nThe browser workflow also covers access/GM failure and success, rate limiting, cookie isolation, live SSE chapter release, named scene checkpoints, replay without mutation, sound/motion preferences, refresh persistence, artifact/map progression, solve/undo, offline pause/resume reconciliation, heartbeat, deep links, responsive viewports, and axe serious/critical violations.\r\n\r\nRequired viewports are 2560 Ã— 1440, 1920 Ã— 1080, 1440 Ã— 900, 430 Ã— 932, 390 Ã— 844, and 844 Ã— 390. Stable screenshots are captured at semantic checkpoints rather than arbitrary delays. Reports, traces, screenshots, and production logs are ignored under `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\validation\\artifacts\\validation` for network-share checkouts.\r\n\r\nFocused commands: `npm test`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, `npm run assets:validate`, `npm run test:e2e`, and `npm run build`. Detailed animation test and manual leak/profile instructions are in `docs/animation/testing.md` and `docs/animation/performance.md`.\r\n\r\nProduct-polish unit coverage verifies public catalog search/progress filtering, no-results reset, connection-failure recovery, shell current-route semantics, skip navigation, mobile-menu focus entry and Escape restoration, and deliberate shell removal on the canonical journal. Manual browser review covers the landing explanation and mobile control placement; public search/no-results behavior; Player empty-library and sign-in states; Captain desktop/mobile grids, tabs, wizard selection/progress, and focus restoration; Studio density; invitation recovery; route metadata; immersive journal entry; console output; and document-width checks at the six required viewports.\r\n\r\nTall Tale Studio unit coverage asserts the complete 23-type block registry, schema metadata, required-field validation, provider dispatch, safe unknown-block behavior, normalization, conditions, variable mutation, route rate limits, slug rules, optimistic autosave tokens, and the helper verification envelope. Browser coverage proves the version-pinned player/Captain/helper path, helper heartbeat and revocation, completed catalog state, dnd-kit authoring affordance, responsive inspector dismissal, isolated block preview, published-version preview, copy-to-new-draft, and a media-rich create/upload/author/align/publish/play/artifact/history golden path. Database verification additionally proves that the additive seed has a current immutable version, two chapters, and a terminal block while retaining legacy campaign data. For a manual golden path: edit and preview the seeded tale, publish, start it from `/tales`, solve `lantern`, approve it from `/captain`, and complete the remaining blocks.\r\n\r\nTall Tale Platform unit coverage adds lifecycle transition rules, role/resource authorization, and audit redaction. Its Chromium journey forks and publishes a Tale, atomically creates and accepts a secure invitation, denies cross-role access and missing Player CSRF, launches the crew, publishes a newer edition, proves the active playthrough remains pinned, completes it, validates the exact-version archive, persists pin/hide preferences, retries acceptance idempotently, and verifies replacement/revocation. A read-only mobile WebKit pass covers the reduced-motion gateway, responsive width, accessibility, and protected API denials. Validation also creates a legacy-shaped playthrough, runs the normal progress-preserving seed, and proves that ID, version, events, timestamps, membership, and reveal history survive backfill.\r\n\r\nCanonical journal unit coverage asserts the typed presentation schema, recursive Player secret removal, mode mapping, stable page IDs/parity, two-page spreads, chapter/current-objective indexes, StPageFlip revision refresh, queued turn locking, and distinct left/right geometry. The platform Chromium journey now enters the physical journal after launch, verifies canonical Player-safe state, proves reading-state persistence does not mutate the current story block, follows the same journal into completed historical mode, and checks that the immutable edition/checksum remain visible while actions are disabled.\r\n\r\nThe 2026-07-17 platform release gate passed 25 Vitest files with 80 tests and 21 Playwright tests with 7 intentionally skipped mobile mutation permutations. Post-browser verification passed before and after the progress-preserving seed, and the production build plus both restart-safety cycles completed successfully.\r\n\r\nThe 2026-07-18 product-polish release gate passed 31 Vitest files with 99 tests and the same 21-test Playwright acceptance matrix with 7 intentional mobile mutation skips. Formatting, ESLint, strict TypeScript, animation assets, migration/seed/backfill invariants, accepted-state verification, the progress-preserving seed rerun, the optimized production build, and both restart-safety cycles also passed.\r\n\r\nName                                                          Length LastWriteTime        \r\n----                                                          ------ -------------        \r\nFull_System_Product_Polish_Implementation_Record.md            18841 7/18/2026 12:03:28 PM\r\nvision_waypoint_external_ar_governing_specification_v1_0.pdf 1022751 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nphase_b_implementation_roadmap_v1_0.pdf                       444805 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nTall_Tale_Studio_Phase_1_Codex_Implementation_Prompt.pdf      599225 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nTall_Tale_Platform_Implementation_Record.md                     7731 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nTall_Tale_Platform_Codex_Implementation_Specification.pdf     571011 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nREADME.md                                                       3166 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nForever_Treasure_Cinematic_Animation_Codex_Prompt.pdf         991085 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\nCanonical_Player_Journal_Implementation_Record.md              16467 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\n.gitignore                                                       684 7/18/2026 12:02:03 PM\r\n\r\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

obvious mechanical loop\n14.5 Physical Interaction Animation\n\nThese include:\n\nPage turns\nMap unfolding\nTab movement\nDrawer movement\nArtifact rotation\nPaper transitions\nSeal breaking\nInk appearing\n\nThese should be tied directly to user actions and remain repeatable.\n\n15. Application-Wide Animation Coverage\n\nAudit every primary screen and define an intentional entry transition.\n\nAt minimum:\n\nLanding/home\nTall Tale Library\nExperience detail\nCaptain Studio\nExperience editor\nSession management\nPlayer lobby\nActive Tall Tale\nChapters\nMap\nArtifacts\nMessages\nCompleted voyage journal\nSettings\nEmpty states\nLoading states\nError states\nReconnecting states\nModals\nConfirmation dialogs\n\nâ€œNo animationâ€ should be an intentional decision, not the result of someone forgetting that the page exists.\n\nDo not apply one identical fade to every page.\n\nUse a coherent motion vocabulary:\n\nBooks open\nPaper slides or settles\nMaps unfold\nInk reveals\nPanels rise subtly\nBrass indicators glide\nMessages appear like delivered correspondence\nArtifacts emerge from shadow\nLarge application pages transition with restrained depth\n\nAvoid cartoonish bouncing, excessive spring overshoot, and generic dashboard animation kits.\n\n16. Route Transition Architecture\n\nCreate a shared route-transition system rather than hand-writing unrelated animations in each page.\n\nThe system should provide:\n\nEnter transition\nExit transition\nRoute-specific variant\nReduced-motion fallback\nCorrect keying\nCleanup\nNested-route compatibility\nLoading-state compatibility\nBrowser back/forward compatibility\n\nFor React and Framer Motion, the conceptual implementation may resemble:\n\n<AnimatePresence mode=\"wait\">\n  <motion.main\n    key={location.key}\n    initial=\"initial\"\n    animate=\"enter\"\n    exit=\"exit\"\n    variants={resolveRouteVariants(location.pathname)}\n  >\n    <Outlet />\n  </motion.main>\n</AnimatePresence>\n\nDo not copy this blindly if it conflicts with the actual router or rendering architecture.\n\nThe important requirement is that route identity changes produce reliable animation lifecycle changes.\n\nFor persistent layouts, key the animated content region rather than unnecessarily remounting global providers or connection state.\n\n17. Navigation and Animation Interaction\n\nWhen switching between:\n\nChapters â†’ Map\nMap â†’ Artifacts\nArtifacts â†’ Messages\nMessages â†’ Chapters\n\nthe global shell should remain stable while the content region transitions.\n\nDo not remount the entire application.\n\nPreserve:\n\nSession connection\nAudio state\nUser identity\nTheme selection\nGlobal navigation\nShared header\nStatus indicators\n\nAnimate only the appropriate region.\n\nSuggested physical metaphors:\n\nChapters opens or settles like a book\nMap unfolds or expands\nArtifacts rise into a curated archive\nMessages slide in like letters placed into the journal\n\nKeep transition duration deliberate and polished.\n\nThe project values animation more than a conventional productivity dashboard would. Do not compress everything into 150 ms because a generic UX article claimed humans will perish if an animation lasts half a second.\n\nAt the same time, preserve responsiveness.\n\nUse layered animation:\n\nImmediate input feedback\nMain transition around 350 to 800 ms depending on complexity\nLonger physical sequences only where they remain interruptible\nNo forced waiting before interaction becomes possible\n18. Reduced Motion and Accessibility\n\nAnimation importance does not eliminate accessibility requirements.\n\nRespect:\n\n@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)\n\nReduced motion should:\n\nRemove large spatial movement\nRemove repeated ambient movement\nReplace complex transitions with fast opacity or state changes\nPreserve all information\nPreserve functionality\nPreserve focus management\n\nDo not disable every transition so abruptly that navigation becomes visually confusing.\n\nTabs must support keyboard navigation.\n\nRequired expectations:\n\nLogical tab order\nVisible focus state\nARIA tab roles where appropriate\nArrow-key movement where using a true tab interface\nEnter/space activation\nScreen-reader labels\nCorrect active-state announcement\nNo focus trapped in hidden overlays\nFocus moves appropriately after route changes\n19. Connection Status Redesign\n\nThe screenshot shows a large reconnecting banner extending across the top of the content.\n\nConnection state is important, but it should not dominate or obscure the experience.\n\nImplement a standardized connection indicator with states such as:\n\nConnected\nReconnecting\nOffline reading\nConnection restored\nConnection failed\n\nRequirements:\n\nPersistent compact status in the header where appropriate\nExpanded detail only when necessary\nRetry action\nClear explanation of whether reading remains available\nNo deceptive â€œupdated just nowâ€ behavior\nNo large content-obscuring overlay\nCorrect timestamps based on real events\nSuccessful reconnection feedback\nAccessible status announcements\n\nThe status must report truthfully.\n\nDo not continuously display â€œreconnectingâ€ merely because the status component became emotionally attached to that word.\n\n20. Responsive Behavior\nDesktop\nHorizontal top tabs\nFull-width content area\nLarge book presentation\nMap given substantial workspace\nArtifact gallery uses available width\nMessages use readable columns\nCaptain navigation remains clear\nTablet\nTabs remain visible\nTabs may scroll horizontally\nContent retains full-page behavior\nBook scales without microscopic text\nMap remains interactive\nNo narrow side overlays\nMobile\nUse a compact top selector or bottom section navigation\nPreserve the four main destinations\nDo not hide them behind several menu levels\nBook may transition to a single-page reading mode if necessary\nMap gets a dedicated viewport\nArtifact details become full-screen sheets or routes\nMessages use a conventional readable conversation layout\n21. State Preservation\n\nWhen moving between subpages, preserve reasonable context.\n\nExamples:\n\nChapters should remember the current page or chapter\nMap should remember zoom and selected location during the session\nArtifacts should remember filters or selected artifact when returning\nMessages should remember the current thread or scroll location where practical\n\nDo not reset everything merely because the route changed.\n\nHowever, entry animations must still replay even when content state is preserved.\n\nAnimation state and content state must not be treated as the same thing.\n\n22. Error and Loading States\n\nEvery full-page subpage needs intentional:\n\nLoading state\nEmpty state\nError state\nOffline state\nPermission-restricted state\n\nThese states must use the active theme.\n\nDo not allow fallback components to revert to a generic blue or white application style.\n\nLoading states should use restrained themed animation and should not fake progress.\n\nError states should clearly provide:\n\nWhat failed\nWhat remains usable\nRetry action\nNavigation escape\nDiagnostic identifier where useful\n23. Testing Requirements\n23.1 Routing Tests\n\nVerify:\n\nDirect navigation to each experience route\nRefresh on every route\nBrowser back and forward\nActive-tab state\nActive session versus completed voyage\nInvalid route fallback\nPermission-restricted route behavior\nTheme persistence across route changes\n23.2 Animation Replay Tests\n\nTest that entry animation occurs:\n\nOn first visit\nAfter navigating away and returning\nAfter browser refresh\nAfter browser back\nAfter browser forward\nAfter switching between experience tabs\nAfter closing and reopening a modal\nAfter reconnecting\nUnder reduced-motion preference\n\nWhere automated visual timing tests would be brittle, test the lifecycle signals, state transitions, and animation attributes.\n\nAlso perform manual verification.\n\n23.3 Theme Tests\n\nVerify every major page under:\n\nVerdant Depths\nMoonlit Blue\nExperience override\nApplication preference\nDefault fallback\nRefresh\nNew session\nCompleted session\nOffline state\nError state\n\nSearch rendered styles and source code for leftover hardcoded blue values from the old Vision Waypoint implementation.\n\n23.4 Port Tests\n\nVerify:\n\nStandard launch uses port 3000\nPort 3000 conflict fails clearly\nNo automatic fallback occurs\nNo source references to 3101 or 3106 remain except migration documentation\nBrowser navigation remains on port 3000\nAPI calls use the intended endpoint\nWebSocket calls use the intended endpoint\nPlaywright and integration tests use port 3000\nProduction build preview follows documented behavior without creating another canonical version\n23.5 Responsive Tests\n\nTest at representative widths:\n\n375 px\n430 px\n768 px\n1024 px\n1440 px\n1920 px\n\nVerify:\n\nTabs remain reachable\nNo content is covered\nBook remains readable\nMap remains usable\nArtifact layouts do not collapse incorrectly\nMessage content remains readable\nCaptain navigation remains understandable\nConnection notices do not obstruct content\n24. Required Documentation\n\nUpdate project documentation to explain:\n\nCanonical frontend entry point\nCanonical development command\nCanonical port\nTheme architecture\nTheme precedence\nExperience routes\nCaptain navigation hierarchy\nAnimation categories\nHow route animations replay\nReduced-motion behavior\nDeprecated ports and prototypes\nAny removed legacy entry points\n\nAdd an architecture note explaining that alternate visual experiments must be implemented as theme options or feature branches, not permanently launched as separate application versions on arbitrary ports.\n\n25. Migration Strategy\n\nPerform the work in this order.\n\nStep 1: Repository and Runtime Audit\nIdentify all current application entry points\nIdentify which code runs on 3000, 3101, and 3106\nDetermine which implementation contains the newest features\nDocument duplicated and divergent code\nIdentify the canonical app\nStep 2: Consolidate Feature Code\nBring the restored book and newest Vision Waypoint functionality into the canonical app\nEliminate reliance on alternate frontend servers\nPreserve working functionality before deleting legacy code\nStep 3: Implement Shared Experience Shell\nShared header\nShared theme context\nShared top tabs\nShared connection status\nShared routed content area\nStep 4: Convert Side Overlays to Full Routes\nChapters\nMap\nArtifacts\nMessages\nStep 5: Implement Semantic Theme System\nVerdant Depths\nMoonlit Blue\nApplication preference\nExperience override\nToken migration\nStep 6: Standardize Captain Navigation\nGlobal shell\nCaptain workspace shell\nBreadcrumbs\nPredictable parent navigation\nActive states\nStep 7: Implement Animation Lifecycle System\nRoute transitions\nComponent transitions\nState-change animation\nAmbient animation\nReduced motion\nCleanup and replay correctness\nStep 8: Port Consolidation\nEnforce port 3000\nRemove obsolete scripts and references\nUpdate tests and documentation\nConfirm no hidden alternate app remains\nStep 9: Verification\nAutomated tests\nManual route pass\nAnimation replay pass\nTheme pass\nResponsive pass\nAccessibility pass\nRuntime and port pass\n\nDo not delete an alternate implementation until its unique functionality has been identified and either merged or intentionally rejected.\n\n26. Acceptance Criteria\n\nThe work is complete only when all of the following are true:\n\nThe interactive book remains available.\nChapters, Map, Artifacts, and Messages appear as prominent top-level experience tabs.\nEach section has a full routed content page.\nThe current right-side overlay tabs are removed as primary navigation.\nThe default application color scheme is the green Verdant Depths theme.\nEvery Vision Waypoint screen uses the same semantic theme system.\nMoonlit Blue can be selected through application settings.\nA Tall Tale can override the application theme.\nCaptain navigation uses one documented hierarchy.\nBrowser back and forward work predictably.\nEvery primary page has an intentional transition.\nRoute entry animations replay after returning to a page.\nRefreshing a page does not permanently suppress animation.\nModals and secondary interactions animate every time they open.\nReduced-motion behavior works.\nThe canonical application runs on port 3000.\nPorts 3101 and 3106 are no longer required or presented as separate versions.\nThe app does not silently switch to another port.\nAutomated tests pass.\nNo major content is obscured by reconnecting or historical-status overlays.\nDesktop, tablet, and mobile layouts remain usable.\nNo existing session, book, map, artifact, or message functionality is silently discarded.\nDocumentation reflects the consolidated architecture.\n27. Required Final Report\n\nAt completion, provide a detailed report containing:\n\nArchitecture Changes\nCanonical application entry point\nShared shell structure\nRoute structure\nTheme architecture\nNavigation hierarchy\nAnimation architecture\nFiles Changed\n\nGroup by:\n\nRouting\nComponents\nThemes\nNavigation\nAnimation\nRuntime configuration\nTests\nDocumentation\nRemoved legacy code\nLegacy Consolidation\n\nExplain:\n\nWhat was running on 3000\nWhat was running on 3101\nWhat was running on 3106\nWhich features differed\nHow the features were merged\nWhich obsolete entry points were removed\nTesting Results\n\nInclude:\n\nUnit tests\nIntegration tests\nEnd-to-end tests\nManual animation replay results\nTheme verification\nResponsive verification\nAccessibility verification\nPort verification\nRemaining Risks\n\nList any unresolved issues honestly.\n\nDo not claim completion if:\n\nSome pages still lack animation\nSome blue hardcoded screens remain\nAlternate ports are still needed\nSide overlays remain the only route to important content\nRefreshing or revisiting suppresses animation\nCaptain navigation still changes structure between screens\n28. Implementation Principle\n\nTreat this work as consolidation of the actual product, not creation of another prototype.\n\nThere must be:\n\nOne application\nOne canonical runtime\nOne navigation language\nOne theme system\nOne motion system\nOne coherent experience\n\nThe product may contain many voyages, visual themes, captain tools, player states, and story formats.\n\nIt must not contain several competing definitions of what the application itself is.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

The root defaults to **Verdant Depths** and `[data-theme=\"moonlit-blue\"]` replaces the semantic values for **Moonlit Blue**. Player and staff application preferences are persisted in their existing JSON preference records and cached locally only as a fallback. A Tall Tale stores `APPLICATION`, `VERDANT_DEPTHS`, or `MOONLIT_BLUE`; an explicit Tale value is scoped to the Experience root and therefore overrides, but never overwrites, the application preference.\n+\n+Product route content is keyed by pathname inside `AnimatePresence`, while the Experience shell keys only its content region. State-change events remain sequence/event-ID driven and are not confused with route entry. The launcher refuses an occupied port unless its state record, runtime root, PID, and listener ownership all match. Isolated validation may override its temporary test port, but no product route, package script, or fallback treats that as another application version. Alternate visual experiments belong in semantic themes or feature branches, not permanent servers on arbitrary ports.\n*** Update File: C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation\\docs\\design-system.md\n@@\n ## Product application foundation\n@@\n System copy uses the shared product vocabulary: a Creator authors a reusable **Tall Tale**, a Captain configures a version-pinned **Voyage**, an invited **Player** waits for launch, and the **Journal** is the canonical in-session and completed-history surface. The collective word _participant_ is used when a role distinction is unnecessary; _crew_ remains optional flavor rather than permission language. Personal names and event-specific language belong to authored content and invitations, not generic product controls.\n+\n+## Semantic application themes\n+\n+All application chrome and operational surfaces consume the `--color-*` aliases in `tokens.css`. **Verdant Depths** is the default deep green-black, muted emerald, warm-brass, cream-parchment scheme. **Moonlit Blue** replaces water, fog, glow, surfaces, borders, text, focus, and navigation values with a controlled navy/cyan family. Vision Waypoint and Companion screens use those same aliases and are not independent blue applications.\n+\n+Theme precedence is: explicit Experience override, persisted Player or staff application preference, then Verdant Depths. `APPLICATION` on a Tall Tale means inherit. [{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n# The Forever Treasure Companion\r\n\r\nProduction-oriented foundation and fully automated local demo for a private nautical-fantasy date-night Tall Tale. All committed story material is generic development seed content; no final surprise content belongs in this public repository.\r\n\r\n## Current phase\r\n\r\nThe application is now a unified Tall Tale platform. The cinematic gateway opens distinct Player, Captain, and Creator workspaces without treating role choice as authorization. Phase B-1 adds persisted, immutable-versioned Vision Waypoints, a strict protocol, platform adapters, an installable PWA shell, and a restricted Electron Windows shell without creating separate Player, Captain, or Studio applications. The authorized B-1 verifier is explicitly deterministic development-only: it does not capture a camera, inspect the game, or perform real vision inference.\r\n\r\nThe original companion and expanded Game Master Command Center remain integrated compatibility surfaces. A shared animation director coordinates the harbor, journal, chart, artifact, gateway, and finale experiences without allowing presentation timing to outrun server truth. All committed content remains fictional development seed material.\r\n\r\n## Stack\r\n\r\n- Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, strict TypeScript\r\n- Prisma 6.19.3 with SQLite for local/test and an equivalent MySQL production schema\r\n- GSAP 3.15 for orchestrated scenes and SVG/text work; Motion 12 for React interaction and presence\r\n- StPageFlip for the journal; local Rive and Lottie runtimes with explicit static fallbacks\r\n- bcrypt password/access-code hashing, server-side database sessions, CSRF tokens, and rate limiting\r\n- Vitest and Playwright\r\n\r\n## Reproducible setup\r\n\r\nRequirements: Windows PowerShell 5.1+, Node.js 24, npm 11, and Git. MySQL 8 is only required for production parity; the supported local/demo/test strategy is SQLite.\r\n\r\n### Fastest Windows demo\r\n\r\nAfter cloning, double-click `Start Forever Treasure Dev.cmd`, or run:\r\n\r\n```powershell\r\nnpm run dev:full\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe launcher creates an ignored `.env` when absent, installs the exact lockfile, generates Prisma, applies migrations, creates generic development data only when the voyage is missing, starts the app, checks health, and prints the URLs and credentials. Existing campaign progress is preserved across normal stop/start cycles. Network/UNC workspaces are mirrored to an ignored local runtime under `%LOCALAPPDATA%` because Node and SQLite are unreliable on network shares. Stop it with `npm run dev:stop`.\r\n\r\n- Role gateway: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/`\r\n- Player: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\r\n- Player library: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/player`\r\n- Tall Tale catalog: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tales`\r\n- Player phrase: `development-moonwake`\r\n- Returning Player development login: `sera` / the configured `PLAYER_PASSWORD` (or `PLAYER_ACCESS_CODE` fallback)\r\n- GM: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\r\n- Studio: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/studio`\r\n- Vision Waypoint Studio: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/studio/vision-waypoints`\r\n- Captain: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/captain`\r\n- GM development[REDACTED: LOGIN CREDENTIAL]\r\n\r\nThese values are disposable local defaults, never production credentials. To opt into LAN access, run `powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/start-dev.ps1 -Lan`; Windows Firewall and network policy still determine whether another device can connect.\r\n\r\n### Portable manual setup\r\n\r\n```bash\r\ngit clone https://github.com/Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT.git\r\ncd treasurehuntSoT\r\nnpm ci\r\ncp .env.example .env\r\nnpm run db:generate\r\nnpm run db:migrate\r\nnpm run db:seed\r\nnpm run dev\r\n```\r\n\r\nSet unique local values in `.env`; the seedâ€™s fallback values are development-only and must never be used outside an isolated development database. Player path: `/tale/development-forever-treasure`. GM path: `/quartermaster`.\r\n\r\n## Commands\r\n\r\n| Command                                   | Purpose                                                               |\r\n| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |\r\n| `npm run dev:full`                        | Idempotent setup, migration, progress-safe seed check, and server     |\r\n| `npm run dev:stop`                        | Safely stop only the recorded development process                     |\r\n| `npm run validate`                        | Fresh DB, format/lint/types/unit/E2E/a11y/build/restart proof         |\r\n| `npm run dev`                             | Next.js only; assumes setup is already complete                       |\r\n| `npm run build`                           | Production build                                                      |\r\n| `npm start`                               | Start production build                                                |\r\n| `npm test`                                | Domain, command-center, Studio, animation, and component unit tests   |\r\n| `npm run assets:validate`                 | Validate local Lottie/Rive contracts and fallback paths               |\r\n| `npm run test:e2e`                        | Cross-browser player, Command Center, Studio, and animation workflows |\r\n| `npm run db:migrate`                      | Apply local SQLite migrations                                         |\r\n| `npm run db:seed`                         | Load replaceable development content                                  |\r\n| `npm run db:preset -- mid-voyage`         | Reset to a repeatable development-only companion preset               |\r\n| `npm run db:generate:mysql`               | Generate production MySQL client                                      |\r\n| `npm run db:migrate:mysql:command-center` | Apply the Phase 3 production MySQL migration                          |\r\n| `npm run db:migrate:mysql:studio`         | Apply the production-parity Tall Tale Studio migration                |\r\n| `npm run db:migrate:mysql:platform`       | Apply the unified Tall Tale Platform migration                        |\r\n| `npm run db:migrate:mysql:vision`         | Apply the additive Phase B-1 MySQL migration                          |\r\n| `npm run desktop:dev`                     | Start the shared Next.js UI in the restricted Electron shell          |\r\n| `npm run desktop:test`                    | Test the desktop command allowlist and input validation               |\r\n| `npm run desktop:build`                   | Build Next standalone and a Windows NSIS installer                    |\r\n\r\nFull setup, validation stages, output locations, clean-clone instructions, and troubleshooting are in [local development](docs/local-development.md) and [testing](docs/testing.md). Phase B-1 architecture, flags, desktop, PWA, protocol, API, security, test, and demonstration instructions are indexed from [the B-1 architecture](Development_Docs/AR/Phase_B1/B1_Architecture.md). Unified identity, invitations, libraries, authorization, history, and migration are in [Tall Tale Platform](docs/tall-tale-platform.md); Studio architecture and operations are in [Tall Tale Studio](docs/tall-tale-studio.md).\r\n\r\n## Repository workflow\r\n\r\n`main` is canonical. At task start: fetch, inspect status/remotes, and pull with rebase when clean. At task end: validate, review the complete diff, commit intentionally, fetch/rebase if needed, push without force, and verify local `HEAD` equals `origin/main`. See [Codex handoff](docs/codex-handoff.md).\r\n\r\n## Important limitations\r\n\r\nThe full story, final Rive artwork/audio, horizontal scale-out for SSE, and production deployment are later work. The current sound is restrained and procedural. The sole third-party visual sample is MIT-licensed, local, documented, and development-only; production uses original SVG/CSS fallbacks. The repository is public, so only generic development content is permitted. See [known limitations](docs/known-limitations.md).\r\n\r\n## Next steps\r\n\r\nAdd CI for the complete validation gate, exercise the parallel MySQL schema in an integration environment, and replace process-local SSE fan-out before multi-instance deployment. Real surprise content remains blocked until repository visibility is verified private.\r\n# Shared development documentation\r\n\r\n`Development_Docs` is the canonical repository location for shared development references, requirements, architecture notes, design decisions, implementation plans, testing notes, meeting notes, and other planning material that should remain available across Codex sessions and computers.\r\n\r\nFiles in this directory are normal Git-tracked project files. The repository's end-of-task synchronization workflow inspects additions, modifications, renames, moves, and deletions here and, after safety checks, can commit and push them through the same approved branch workflow used for `Codex_Chats`. The files are not copied into the conversation archive or packaged into a second store.\r\n\r\n## Working from another computer\r\n\r\nBefore relying on or editing these documents:\r\n\r\n1. Confirm that this is the intended repository and branch with `git remote -v`, `git branch --show-current`, and `git status --short --branch`.\r\n2. Preserve and review any local modifications. Do not reset, clean, restore, or force-push them away.\r\n3. Run `git fetch --prune origin`, then compare the branch with its upstream using `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\r\n4. If the branch is only behind and the working tree is clean, update it with `git pull --ff-only`. If it is ahead, diverged, or locally modified, reconcile the work deliberately before editing these documents.\r\n5. If Git reports a conflict, preserve both versions and resolve it visibly. Do not push unresolved conflict markers. For an unmergeable binary document, retain clearly named local and remote variants for review when repository conventions permit.\r\n\r\nThis pull-before-edit check matters: a Codex session on a different computer must synchronize the latest repository state before treating local `Development_Docs` content as current.\r\n\r\n## Safety and change behavior\r\n\r\nDo not store secrets, credentials, private keys, access or API tokens, production database exports, private authentication caches, or sensitive company information here unless both the repository and the specific contents have been explicitly approved for that purpose. This repository may be publicly visible. Temporary, recovery, credential-shaped, and obvious secret files are ignored, and the synchronizer also checks changed file names, bounded textual content, and file sizes before staging.\r\n\r\nDeleting a tracked file locally and committing that deletion also removes it from the GitHub version after the commit is pushed. Git history can still preserve earlier versions of documents that were changed or deleted, subject to the repository's retention and history policies.\r\n\r\nPreserve authored formatting and native file types. Do not rewrite a document merely to normalize it, and do not use this directory as a dumping ground for terminal output, build artifacts, dependency archives, database dumps, application installers, or routine generated test results.\r\n\r\nGit tracks files rather than empty directories. A new subdirectory is synchronized automatically when it contains an eligible file; use an intentional `.gitkeep` only when preserving an otherwise empty directory is genuinely necessary.\r\n# Local development\r\n\r\n## One-command Windows startup\r\n\r\nDouble-click `Start Forever Treasure Dev.cmd` or run `npm run dev:full`. The idempotent launcher:\r\n\r\n1. Finds Node/npm and explains what is missing when neither a normal installation nor the bundled Codex runtime is available.\r\n2. Creates `.env` only when absent; existing local configuration is preserved and validated.\r\n3. Mirrors network-share checkouts to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\development` while keeping dependencies, databases, logs, and generated artifacts out of Git.\r\n4. Runs `npm ci` only when `package-lock.json` changed or dependencies are missing.\r\n5. Generates Prisma, applies the versioned SQLite migrations, and ensures the development fixture exists without replacing an existing campaign. Local access hashes are refreshed from `.env`, while voyage progress, history, staging, and audit state remain intact.\r\n6. Refuses to take an occupied port, starts the server as a hidden recorded process, and waits for an HTTP health response.\r\n7. Prints the player and GM URLs, disposable credentials, and stop command.\r\n\r\nRun `npm run dev:stop` before switching branches or replacing the runtime. Shutdown verifies the recorded PID is the expected Next.js development process before stopping it.\r\n\r\n## URLs and credentials\r\n\r\n- Unified role gateway: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/`\r\n- Player URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\r\n- Player identity/library: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/player`\r\n- Invitation phrase: `development-moonwake`\r\n- Returning Player username: `sera`; password is `PLAYER_PASSWORD`, falling back to `PLAYER_ACCESS_CODE` in an isolated development database\r\n- GM URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\r\n- GM username/password: `kato` / `development-captain-only`\r\n- Animation showcase: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/dev/animations` (development only)\r\n- Tall Tale catalog: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tales`\r\n- Creator Studio: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/studio/library`\r\n- Captain command/library: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/captain/library`\r\n\r\nThe launcher-generated `.env` is ignored. Change these values for any shared environment. Never reuse them for deployment.\r\n\r\n## LAN opt-in\r\n\r\n`powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/start-dev.ps1 -Lan` binds Next.js to `0.0.0.0` and prints the best detected IPv4 URLs. LAN mode is intentionally not the one-click default because it exposes a development server and disposable credentials to the local network. If another device cannot connect, allow the selected TCP port through Windows Firewall and confirm that corporate Wi-Fi/client-isolation policy permits peer traffic.\r\n\r\n## Portable fallback\r\n\r\nOn macOS/Linux or when automation is unavailable:\r\n\r\n```bash\r\nnpm ci\r\ncp .env.example .env\r\nnpm run db:generate\r\nnpm run db:migrate\r\nnpm run db:seed\r\nnpm run dev\r\n```\r\n\r\nUse Node 24 and npm 11.9.0; `.nvmrc`, `.node-version`, `packageManager`, and `package-lock.json` define the reproducible toolchain.\r\n\r\n## Animation development\r\n\r\nOpen `/dev/animations` or use the development-only `TEST ANIMATIONS` button. The lab contains all registered scenes, a no-API trailer, transport/scrub/speed/mode controls, StPageFlip book, live Rive input proof, Lottie controls, fallback states, and local FPS/long-task/runtime metrics. The route intentionally returns 404 from a production server.\r\n\r\nUse `npm run assets:validate` after any asset-contract change. Keep all runtime files and fallbacks under `public`, add provenance to `docs/asset-licenses.md`, and never use a remote CDN placeholder. See `docs/animation/showcase.md` for the manual pass and `docs/animation/` for scene/asset extension contracts.\r\n\r\n## Tall Tale Studio development\r\n\r\nThe seed creates `development-studio-voyage`, its editable draft, and published version 1.0 without replacing the original campaign seed. Sign in through Quartermaster and use Studio to edit it. Asset originals and generated derivatives live under ignored `.data/tall-tale-assets` by default. Override that location with an absolute `TALL_TALE_ASSET_ROOT`; set the upload ceiling with `TALL_TALE_MAX_UPLOAD_MB`.\r\n\r\nDraft preview sessions are explicitly marked and may read draft assets only through an authenticated Creator session. Real Player playthroughs normally begin with a Captain invitation. The development catalog compatibility route remains available, but `/player/*`, `/captain/*`, and `/studio/*` are the canonical role workspaces. Every real playthrough pins itself to an immutable published version. Normal `--ensure` startup also adds missing platform roles/Player credentials and backfills memberships/reveal history for legacy Tall Tale sessions without resetting progress.\r\n\r\n## Clean-clone continuation\r\n\r\nAn authorized session on another computer needs only Git, Node/npm, and this repository. Clone `main`, copy `.env.example` or use the Windows launcher, then run `npm run validate`. No database, generated Prisma client, Playwright browser state, artifact, or machine path is required from the originating computer.\r\n\r\n## Troubleshooting\r\n\r\n- **Port 3000 is occupied:** identify and stop the verified owning application, then rerun the launcher. Startup fails clearly and never selects port 3001, 3101, 3106, or another fallback; it also never kills an unknown listener.\r\n- **Existing `.env` is incomplete:** the launcher preserves it and identifies each missing key. Add the key; it will not overwrite local secrets.\r\n- **Startup fails:** inspect `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\ForeverTreasureCompanion\\development\\.forever\\logs`.\r\n- **Resetting disposable data is explicit:** `npm run db:preset -- awaiting-first-release` intentionally replaces the development fixture. Normal `npm run dev:stop` / `npm run dev:full` cycles preserve it. For validation, `npm run validate` always rebuilds `validation.db` from migrations.\r\n- **Browser binaries are missing:** `npm run validate` installs pinned Chromium and WebKit builds automatically.\r\n- **UNC/network checkout is slow:** the first mirror/install is expected to take longer; subsequent runs retain the dependency cache and only synchronize project files.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

The cinematic gateway opens distinct Player, Captain, and Creator workspaces without treating role choice as authorization. Phase B-1 adds persisted, immutable-versioned Vision Waypoints, a strict protocol, platform adapters, an installable PWA shell, and a restricted Electron Windows shell without creating separate Player, Captain, or Studio applications. The authorized B-1 verifier is explicitly deterministic development-only: it does not capture a camera, inspect the game, or perform real vision inference.\n+The application is now a unified Tall Tale platform. The cinematic gateway opens distinct Player, Captain, and Creator workspaces without treating role choice as authorization. Active and completed voyages share routed Chapters, Map, Artifacts, and Messages pages around the preserved physical book. Verdant Depths is the semantic default theme and Moonlit Blue is a persisted application option; individual Tall Tales may inherit or override that preference. Phase B-1 adds persisted, immutable-versioned Vision Waypoints, a strict protocol, platform adapters, an installable PWA shell, and a restricted Electron Windows shell without creating separate Player, Captain, or Studio applications. The authorized B-1 verifier is explicitly deterministic development-only: it does not capture a camera, inspect the game, or perform real vision inference.\n@@\n The launcher creates an ignored `.env` when absent, installs the exact lockfile, generates Prisma, applies migrations, creates generic development data only when the voyage is missing, starts the app, checks health, and prints the URLs and credentials. Existing campaign progress is preserved across normal stop/start cycles. Network/UNC workspaces are mirrored to an ignored local runtime under `%LOCALAPPDATA%` because Node and SQLite are unreliable on network shares. Stop it with `npm run dev:stop`.\n+\n+Port 3000 is strict and canonical. If another process owns it, startup reports the conflict and stops; it never falls back to 3001, 3101, 3106, or another hidden application version.\n*** Update File: C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation\\docs\\local-development.md\n@@\n # Local development\n+\n+`src/app/layout.tsx` is the canonical frontend entry point. All Player, Captain, Creator, Vision, settings, and Experience routes are part of this Next.js application; alternate themes are token sets, not separate frontends. The canonical local origin is `http://127.0.0.1:3000`.\n@@\n Run `npm run dev:stop` before switching branches or replacing the runtime. Shutdown verifies the recorded PID is the expected Next.js development process before stopping it.\n+\n+The recorded-state fast path also verifies runtime root, canonical port, and socket ownership. A stale PID or a listener from another checkout therefore produces a diagnostic instead of a false “already running” result. Visual experiments must be developed as semantic themes or feature branches and merged back into this entry point; ports 3101 and 3106 are deprecated historical worktree choices, not supported runtimes.\n*** End Patch";

const patch = "*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation\\Development_Docs\\Unified_Experience_Shell_Consolidation_Implementation_Record.md\n+# Unified Experience Shell Consolidation Implementation Record\n+\n+Date: 2026-07-18  \n+Implementation branch: `codex/unified-experience-shell`  \n+Canonical repository: `Kgray44/treasurehuntSoT`\n+\n+## Scope and integration safety\n+\n+This branch consolidates the cumulative Phase B1-B6 product work, the restored physical journal, routed Experience sections, semantic themes, product navigation, strict port-3000 startup, and route-lifecycle hooks into the canonical Next.js application. It was created as a separate Git worktree because other Codex tasks were concurrently polishing product wording and rebuilding application-wide animation. Their main worktree, processes, index, and uncommitted files were not modified or stopped.\n+\n+This separation is intentional integration safety, not a second product. The deliverable has one `src/app/layout.tsx`, one Next.js route tree, and one canonical runtime configuration. Before this branch is merged, compare its small shared-motion overlap in `ProductShell.tsx` and `TallTaleJournalSession.tsx` with the animation-overhaul task; retain the route identity, persistent-provider, reduced-motion, and focus contracts while choosing the newer motion vocabulary where both branches touched the same lines.\n+\n+## Architecture changes\n+\n+### Canonical application entry point and runtime\n+\n+- `src/app/layout.tsx` is the only browser application root.\n+- `npm run dev:full` is the canonical setup/start command; `npm run dev` is the prepared-checkout shortcut.\n+- Development and production scripts bind explicitly to `127.0.0.1:3000`.\n+- `scripts/start-dev.ps1` has no alternate-port parameter or fallback. A port conflict fails clearly, and the “already running” path now requires matching runtime root, recorded port, PID, listener ownership, and HTTP health.\n+- Playwright and the full validation gate default to port 3000. Explicit environment overrides are reserved for disposable validation isolation and are not product runtime choices.\n+\n+### Shared shell and route structure\n+\n+`ThemeProvider`, `AnimationProvider`, and `ProductShell` are long-lived root providers. Role-aware global navigation remains outside nested workspace and Experience navigation.\n+\n+Active compatibility routes:\n+\n+- `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/chapters`\n+- `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/map`\n+- `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/artifacts`\n+- `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/messages`\n+\n+Durable Player and completed-history routes:\n+\n+- `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal/chapters`\n+- `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal/map`\n+- `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal/artifacts`\n+- `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal/messages`\n+\n+Base and archive URLs redirect to Chapters. Invalid section segments use the framework not-found boundary. Both families render one persistent `TallTaleJournalSession`; child pages validate route identity and do not duplicate the Experience implementation.\n+\n+### Experience pages\n+\n+- **Chapters** retains `PhysicalJournalBook`, two-page spreads, page turns, chapter tabs, edition/endpaper pages, current-page persistence, and read-only completed mode.\n+- **Map** is a full chart with released markers, current/completed/released legend, selected-location detail, zoom, and a route back to the relevant book leaf.\n+- **Artifacts** is a responsive gallery and inspection layout with imagery, discovery state, chapter association, timestamp, annotation, and privacy-preserving sealed empty state.\n+- **Messages** is a full history/letter layout with selection, read/unread persistence, timestamps, chapter association, imagery, and readable empty state.\n+- The former right-side section buttons and essential drawer layout were removed. A horizontal parchment/brass tablist drives real routes and remains scrollable at narrow widths.\n+- Connection state is compact in the header and expands only to a non-overlay inline notice when not live. The objective and historical checksum sit in normal document flow below content.\n+\n+### Theme architecture\n+\n+`src/styles/tokens.css` owns one semantic layer, including background, surface, border, text, accent, state, parchment, brass, glow, water, fog, and navigation aliases.\n+\n+- **Verdant Depths** is the root/default green-black maritime palette.\n+- **Moonlit Blue** is a selectable navy/cyan semantic override.\n+- Application preferences persist in `PlayerProfile.preferences` or `GameMasterUser.preferences`; browser storage is only an offline/anonymous fallback.\n+- Tall Tales support `APPLICATION`, `VERDANT_DEPTHS`, and `MOONLIT_BLUE` in new/edit settings.\n+- Precedence is explicit Tale override, persisted application preference, then Verdant Depths.\n+- The Tale override is a scoped `data-theme` on the Experience root, so leaving the route reveals the unchanged application preference.\n+- Library, shell, platform, Vision Waypoint, Companion, forms, settings, statuses, and surrounding atmosphere use the shared semantic values. Stable parchment pigments and media-black preview surfaces remain intentional physical/material values.\n+\n+### Navigation hierarchy\n+\n+1. `ProductShell`: role-aware application workspace destinations and active state.\n+2. Captain/Creator workspace: stable Library and Tall Tale authoring sections.\n+3. Experience: Chapters, Map, Artifacts, Messages.\n+4. Context: page turns, marker/detail controls, filters, and actions.\n+\n+Nested Studio and Captain pages add explicit breadcrumbs to known parents. Player History and Captain Active Sessions have stable first-class routes. Back links use explicit parent URLs; browser history remains normal because tab navigation uses real links/routes.\n+\n+### Animation architecture and lifecycle\n+\n+- The global ProductShell remains mounted while a pathname-keyed content wrapper enters/exits.\n+- The Experience layout remains mounted while its pathname-keyed tabpanel transitions; session identity, SSE, theme, audio/motion state, reading context, and header remain stable.\n+- Libraries settle vertically, Studio scales subtly, Captain pages move laterally, settings settle, Chapters uses the book, Map unfolds, Artifacts rise, and Messages arrive like correspondence.\n+- Returning, refreshing, browser history, and tab changes produce a new route identity rather than relying on one-time session flags.\n+- State-change animation still depends on ordered event/sequence identity and is not replayed merely because a route mounted.\n+- Reduced motion replaces spatial transforms with fast opacity and preserves focus transfer and state announcements.\n+\n+The app-wide animation overhaul running concurrently may supersede variant details. The consolidation contract that must survive integration is: keyed content regions, persistent providers/connections, cleanup via React presence/unmount behavior, event-ID state changes, and reduced-motion/focus semantics.\n+\n+## Files changed by responsibility\n+\n+### Routing\n+\n+- `src/lib/experience-routes.ts`\n+- active session layout, base redirect, section validators, and legacy journal redirects below `src/app/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/`\n+- durable Player journal layout, base redirect, section validator, and archive redirect below `src/app/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/`\n+- `src/app/player/history/page.tsx`\n+- `src/app/captain/sessions/page.tsx`\n+- application/role settings pages\n+\n+### Components\n+\n+- `TallTaleJournalSession.tsx`\n+- `ExperienceSectionPages.tsx`\n+- `PlayerLibrary.tsx`\n+- `PlayerVoyageRoom.tsx`\n+- `ThemeProvider.tsx`\n+- `ThemeSettings.tsx`\n+\n+### Themes and persistence\n+\n+- `src/styles/tokens.css`, `shell.css`, `tall-tale.css`, `platform.css`, `vision.css`, and `companion.css`\n+- `src/theme/theme.ts`\n+- `/api/preferences/theme`\n+- both Prisma schemas and additive SQLite/MySQL migrations\n+- `NewTaleForm.tsx`, `TaleEditor.tsx`, and preference readers in `src/platform/libraries.ts`\n+\n+### Navigation and animation\n+\n+- `ProductShell.tsx`\n+- persistent Experience layout and tabpanel route transitions in `TallTaleJournalSession.tsx`\n+- related shell/Experience CSS\n+\n+### Runtime configuration\n+\n+- `package.json`\n+- `playwright.config.ts`\n+- `scripts/start-dev.ps1`\n+- `scripts/test-all.ps1`\n+\n+### Tests\n+\n+- Experience route and theme preference unit tests\n+- full-page Map/Artifacts/Messages component tests\n+- ProductShell navigation/breadcrumb/transition tests\n+- journal reading-state defaults\n+- Tall Tale Platform E2E direct-route and tab checks\n+\n+### Documentation\n+\n+- `README.md`\n+- `docs/architecture.md`\n+- `docs/design-system.md`\n+- `docs/journal-system.md`\n+- `docs/local-development.md`\n+- `docs/testing.md`\n+- this implementation record\n+\n+## Legacy consolidation and runtime audit\n+\n+| Port | Audit result | Consolidation decision |\n+| --- | --- | --- |\n+| 3000 | Canonical Next.js application from the main checkout | Retained as the only supported frontend origin |\n+| 3101 | A verified Next.js process from the Phase B2 worktree during audit | Its cumulative unique B1-B6 code was merged from the Phase B6 descendant; the process/worktree was not stopped because concurrent work owned it |\n+| 3106 | No listener during audit and no source runtime reference | No implementation or entry point to retain |\n+\n+The B1-B6 branches were a linear cumulative implementation sequence. Merging the Phase B6 tip therefore brought the restored book, Vision foundation/capture/authoring/runtime/integration/release work into this single route tree without copying another app. Source references that launched or defaulted to alternate frontend ports were removed. No phase worktree or branch was deleted: they remain Git history and may still be in use by another task, but the canonical app does not depend on them.\n+\n+Removed or replaced legacy behavior:\n+\n+- right-side vertical section navigation and essential side drawers;\n+- base journal URLs as terminal pages (now explicit Chapters redirects);\n+- alternate/fallback frontend port selection;\n+- Vision/Companion hardcoded root-blue surfaces as an independent theme family;\n+- duplicated active/completed Experience renderers;\n+- ambiguous stale-state “already running” success.\n+\n+## Verification record\n+\n+Focused validation completed before the release gate:\n+\n+- Prisma client generation: passed.\n+- TypeScript strict typecheck: passed.\n+- ESLint: passed after resolving the section-gallery ARIA warning.\n+- Vitest: 44 files, 135 tests passed before the final documentation pass.\n+- PowerShell 5.1 parser check: required before final handoff.\n+- Full isolated runtime validation, E2E, production build/restart, responsive/theme/accessibility browser review, strict port conflict proof, and final counts: recorded after the final gate.\n+\n+## Remaining integration risks\n+\n+- The app-wide animation overhaul and wording-polish tasks are concurrent. Their uncommitted main-worktree changes were deliberately not consumed after this branch was created. Merge by comparing overlapping shell/motion/copy files; do not blindly choose either side wholesale.\n+- The externally running 3101 worktree process was not killed. It is not required by this branch, but its owner must stop it when that task is finished.\n+- Application themes persist per Player or staff profile. Anonymous public pages correctly use browser-local fallback because they have no authenticated server identity.\n+- Locked Player content remains unnamed by design. Full-page Map/Artifact empty states preserve the secret boundary rather than inventing or leaking unreleased labels.\n*** End Patch";
