simply call browser history without knowing where the user should logically return.\n\nPrefer explicit parent-route navigation when entering from structured workflows.\n\nBrowser back must still work normally.\n\n11. Canonical Runtime Consolidation\n\nThe project must run as one canonical application on port 3000.\n\nCurrent alternate ports such as 3101 and 3106 indicate fragmented or temporary implementations.\n\nConsolidate them.\n\n11.1 Required Result\n\nThe normal development command must launch the full canonical application at:\n\nhttp://localhost:3000\n\nOnly one frontend instance should be required.\n\nAll current features must be accessible from that single application.\n\n11.2 Repository Audit\n\nSearch the repository for:\n\n3000\n3101\n3106\nPORT=\nlocalhost:\n127.0.0.1:\nvite.config\nnext.config\nwebpack\ndevServer\npreview\ndemo\nvision-waypoint\nprototype\nlegacy\n\nIdentify:\n\nAlternate frontend entry points\nDuplicate applications\nOld demos\nTemporary Vite servers\nHardcoded URLs\nProxy configuration\nEnvironment files\nPackage scripts\nDocumentation\nTest configuration\nPlaywright base URLs\nAPI CORS configuration\nWebSocket origins\nDevelopment launch scripts\n\nDetermine which implementation is canonical.\n\nMerge any newer work from alternate versions into the canonical application rather than preserving alternate servers.\n\n11.3 Remove Legacy Runtime Paths\n\nAfter confirming feature parity:\n\nRemove obsolete package scripts that launch 3101 or 3106\nRemove or archive obsolete demo entry points\nRemove hardcoded links to alternate ports\nUpdate API and WebSocket configuration\nUpdate tests to target port 3000\nUpdate README and developer documentation\nUpdate environment examples\nUpdate launch scripts\nUpdate Codex development instructions\nEnsure the application does not silently fall back to another port\n\nIf port 3000 is already occupied, startup should fail with a clear message.\n\nDo not automatically choose port 3001, 3101, 3106, or another random port. Silent fallback conceals duplicate running instances and causes exactly this kind of confusion.\n\nUse strict-port configuration where supported.\n\nFor Vite, this may resemble:\n\nserver: {\n  port: 3000,\n  strictPort: true\n}\n\nUse the equivalent mechanism for the actual framework.\n\n11.4 Process Cleanup\n\nDocument and, where safe, automate how to stop obsolete development processes.\n\nDuring implementation:\n\nIdentify processes currently listening on 3000, 3101, and 3106\nDo not kill unrelated processes blindly\nStop only verified project development servers\nConfirm that only the canonical application remains\nConfirm that refreshing the browser never redirects to another port\n\nThe final report must state which obsolete runtime paths were removed.\n\n12. Animation Is a Core Product System\n\nAnimation is not decoration in this application.\n\nIt communicates:\n\nStory progression\nNavigation context\nPhysicality\nPage movement\nDiscovery\nUnlocking\nMap revelation\nArtifact acquisition\nMessage arrival\nChanges in state\nThe relationship between the digital interface and a physical captainâ€™s journal\n\nAnimations must not run only once per browser session unless the behavior is intentionally specified as a one-time story event.\n\nRoute entry and interface transition animations must replay whenever their triggering condition occurs.\n\n13. Current Animation Failure Modes to Investigate\n\nAudit the codebase for common causes of animation running only once:\n\nPersistent hasAnimated flags\nModule-level animation state\nLocal storage animation flags\nSession storage animation flags\nComponents never unmounting between routes\nRoute components sharing the same React key\nMissing AnimatePresence\nIncorrect initial={false}\nAnimation state stuck at \"visible\"\nIntersection observers configured with triggerOnce: true\nScroll observers never reset\nCSS classes added once and never removed\nGSAP timelines created once and reused incorrectly\nTimelines not reverted during cleanup\nEvent listeners not reattached\nMotion values retained across navigation\nCached layouts bypassing entry transitions\nSuspense/loading boundaries swallowing animation mounts\nReduced-motion state accidentally applied globally\nPage transitions implemented only in one layout branch\nAnimations triggered exclusively by initial application boot\n\nCorrect the underlying lifecycle behavior.\n\nDo not merely add artificial delays or force a complete browser reload.\n\n14. Animation Lifecycle Rules\n\nClassify animations into explicit categories.\n\n14.1 Route Entry Animations\n\nThese should run whenever the user enters a route, including:\n\nInitial load\nNavigation from another route\nReturning to a previous route\nBrowser back\nBrowser forward\nRe-entering a section from another tab\n\nExamples:\n\nLibrary arrival\nCaptain Studio arrival\nChapters opening\nMap unfolding\nArtifact archive opening\nMessages appearing\nCompleted journal opening\n\nUse the routerâ€™s location key or equivalent route identity to ensure transitions replay correctly.\n\n14.2 Component Reveal Animations\n\nThese should run whenever the component is newly presented.\n\nExamples:\n\nModal opening\nDrawer opening\nArtifact inspection\nChapter selector expansion\nNotification appearing\nMap marker details\nMessage thread opening\n\nThey must replay each time the component opens.\n\n14.3 State-Change Animations\n\nThese run when a meaningful state transition occurs.\n\nExamples:\n\nArtifact unlocked\nChapter completed\nMap location revealed\nMessage received\nObjective changed\nConnection restored\nSession completed\n\nThese must not replay merely because the page refreshed unless the product specifically intends to reconstruct the event.\n\nUse transition identity or event IDs to distinguish genuine new state changes from page mounting.\n\n14.4 Continuous Ambient Animation\n\nThese may run continuously or loop gently.\n\nExamples:\n\nFog drift\nWater shimmer\nDust particles\nCandlelight\nVery subtle glow movement\nBackground star movement\n\nAmbient effects must:\n\nAvoid harming readability\nAvoid excessive GPU use\nPause or simplify when the tab is hidden\nRespect reduced-motion preferences\nAvoid synchronizing into an obvious mechanical loop\n14.5 Physical Interaction Animation\n\nThese include:\n\nPage turns\nMap unfolding\nTab movement\nDrawer movement\nArtifact rotation\nPaper transitions\nSeal breaking\nInk appearing\n\nThese should be tied directly to user actions and remain repeatable.\n\n15. Application-Wide Animation Coverage\n\nAudit every primary screen and define an intentional entry transition.\n\nAt minimum:\n\nLanding/home\nTall Tale Library\nExperience detail\nCaptain Studio\nExperience editor\nSession management\nPlayer lobby\nActive Tall Tale\nChapters\nMap\nArtifacts\nMessages\nCompleted voyage journal\nSettings\nEmpty states\nLoading states\nError states\nReconnecting states\nModals\nConfirmation dialogs\n\nâ€œNo animationâ€ should be an intentional decision, not the result of someone forgetting that the page exists.\n\nDo not apply one identical fade to every page.\n\nUse a coherent motion vocabulary:\n\nBooks open\nPaper slides or settles\nMaps unfold\nInk reveals\nPanels rise subtly\nBrass indicators glide\nMessages appear like delivered correspondence\nArtifacts emerge from shadow\nLarge application pages transition with restrained depth\n\nAvoid cartoonish bouncing, excessive spring overshoot, and generic dashboard animation kits.\n\n16. Route Transition Architecture\n\nCreate a shared route-transition system rather than hand-writing unrelated animations in each page.\n\nThe system should provide:\n\nEnter transition\nExit transition\nRoute-specific variant\nReduced-motion fallback\nCorrect keying\nCleanup\nNested-route compatibility\nLoading-state compatibility\nBrowser back/forward compatibility\n\nFor React and Framer Motion, the conceptual implementation may resemble:\n\n<AnimatePresence mode=\"wait\">\n  <motion.main\n    key={location.key}\n    initial=\"initial\"\n    animate=\"enter\"\n    exit=\"exit\"\n    variants={resolveRouteVariants(location.pathname)}\n  >\n    <Outlet />\n  </motion.main>\n</AnimatePresence>\n\nDo not copy this blindly if it conflicts with the actual router or rendering architecture.\n\nThe important requirement is that route identity changes produce reliable animation lifecycle changes.\n\nFor persistent layouts, key the animated content region rather than unnecessarily remounting global providers or connection state.\n\n17. Navigation and Animation Interaction\n\nWhen switching between:\n\nChapters â†’ Map\nMap â†’ Artifacts\nArtifacts â†’ Messages\nMessages â†’ Chapters\n\nthe global shell should remain stable while the content region transitions.\n\nDo not remount the entire application.\n\nPreserve:\n\nSession connection\nAudio state\nUser identity\nTheme selection\nGlobal navigation\nShared header\nStatus indicators\n\nAnimate only the appropriate region.\n\nSuggested physical metaphors:\n\nChapters opens or settles like a book\nMap unfolds or expands\nArtifacts rise into a curated archive\nMessages slide in like letters placed into the journal\n\nKeep transition duration deliberate and polished.\n\nThe project values animation more than a conventional productivity dashboard would. Do not compress everything into 150 ms because a generic UX article claimed humans will perish if an animation lasts half a second.\n\nAt the same time, preserve responsiveness.\n\nUse layered animation:\n\nImmediate input feedback\nMain transition around 350 to 800 ms depending on complexity\nLonger physical sequences only where they remain interruptible\nNo forced waiting before interaction becomes possible\n18. Reduced Motion and Accessibility\n\nAnimation importance does not eliminate accessibility requirements.\n\nRespect:\n\n@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)\n\nReduced motion should:\n\nRemove large spatial movement\nRemove repeated ambient movement\nReplace complex transitions with fast opacity or state changes\nPreserve all information\nPreserve functionality\nPreserve focus management\n\nDo not disable every transition so abruptly that navigation becomes visually confusing.\n\nTabs must support keyboard navigation.\n\nRequired expectations:\n\nLogical tab order\nVisible focus state\nARIA tab roles where appropriate\nArrow-key movement where using a true tab interface\nEnter/space activation\nScreen-reader labels\nCorrect active-state announcement\nNo focus trapped in hidden overlays\nFocus moves appropriately after route changes\n19. Connection Status Redesign\n\nThe screenshot shows a large reconnecting banner extending across the top of the content.\n\nConnection state is important, but it should not dominate or obscure the experience.\n\nImplement a standardized connection indicator with states such as:\n\nConnected\nReconnecting\nOffline reading\nConnection restored\nConnection failed\n\nRequirements:\n\nPersistent compact status in the header where appropriate\nExpanded detail only when necessary\nRetry action\nClear explanation of whether reading remains available\nNo deceptive â€œupdated just nowâ€ behavior\nNo large content-obscuring overlay\nCorrect timestamps based on real events\nSuccessful reconnection feedback\nAccessible status announcements\n\nThe status must report truthfully.\n\nDo not continuously display â€œreconnectingâ€ merely because the status component became emotionally attached to that word.\n\n20. Responsive Behavior\nDesktop\nHorizontal top tabs\nFull-width content area\nLarge book presentation\nMap given substantial workspace\nArtifact gallery uses available width\nMessages use readable columns\nCaptain navigation remains clear\nTablet\nTabs remain visible\nTabs may scroll horizontally\nContent retains full-page behavior\nBook scales without microscopic text\nMap remains interactive\nNo narrow side overlays\nMobile\nUse a compact top selector or bottom section navigation\nPreserve the four main destinations\nDo not hide them behind several menu levels\nBook may transition to a single-page reading mode if necessary\nMap gets a dedicated viewport\nArtifact details become full-screen sheets or routes\nMessages use a conventional readable conversation layout\n21. State Preservation\n\nWhen moving between subpages, preserve reasonable context.\n\nExamples:\n\nChapters should remember the current page or chapter\nMap should remember zoom and selected location during the session\nArtifacts should remember filters or selected artifact when returning\nMessages should remember the current thread or scroll location where practical\n\nDo not reset everything merely because the route changed.\n\nHowever, entry animations must still replay even when content state is preserved.\n\nAnimation state and content state must not be treated as the same thing.\n\n22. Error and Loading States\n\nEvery full-page subpage needs intentional:\n\nLoading state\nEmpty state\nError state\nOffline state\nPermission-restricted state\n\nThese states must use the active theme.\n\nDo not allow fallback components to revert to a generic blue or white application style.\n\nLoading states should use restrained themed animation and should not fake progress.\n\nError states should clearly provide:\n\nWhat failed\nWhat remains usable\nRetry action\nNavigation escape\nDiagnostic identifier where useful\n23. Testing Requirements\n23.1 Routing Tests\n\nVerify:\n\nDirect navigation to each experience route\nRefresh on every route\nBrowser back and forward\nActive-tab state\nActive session versus completed voyage\nInvalid route fallback\nPermission-restricted route behavior\nTheme persistence across route changes\n23.2 Animation Replay Tests\n\nTest that entry animation occurs:\n\nOn first visit\nAfter navigating away and returning\nAfter browser refresh\nAfter browser back\nAfter browser forward\nAfter switching between experience tabs\nAfter closing and reopening a modal\nAfter reconnecting\nUnder reduced-motion preference\n\nWhere automated visual timing tests would be brittle, test the lifecycle signals, state transitions, and animation attributes.\n\nAlso perform manual verification.\n\n23.3 Theme Tests\n\nVerify every major page under:\n\nVerdant Depths\nMoonlit Blue\nExperience override\nApplication preference\nDefault fallback\nRefresh\nNew session\nCompleted session\nOffline state\nError state\n\nSearch rendered styles and source code for leftover hardcoded blue values from the old Vision Waypoint implementation.\n\n23.4 Port Tests\n\nVerify:\n\nStandard launch uses port 3000\nPort 3000 conflict fails clearly\nNo automatic fallback occurs\nNo source references to 3101 or 3106 remain except migration documentation\nBrowser navigation remains on port 3000\nAPI calls use the intended endpoint\nWebSocket calls use the intended endpoint\nPlaywright and integration tests use port 3000\nProduction build preview follows documented behavior without creating another canonical version\n23.5 Responsive Tests\n\nTest at representative widths:\n\n375 px\n430 px\n768 px\n1024 px\n1440 px\n1920 px\n\nVerify:\n\nTabs remain reachable\nNo content is covered\nBook remains readable\nMap remains usable\nArtifact layouts do not collapse incorrectly\nMessage content remains readable\nCaptain navigation remains understandable\nConnection notices do not obstruct content\n24. Required Documentation\n\nUpdate project documentation to explain:\n\nCanonical frontend entry point\nCanonical development command\nCanonical port\nTheme architecture\nTheme precedence\nExperience routes\nCaptain navigation hierarchy\nAnimation categories\nHow route animations replay\nReduced-motion behavior\nDeprecated ports and prototypes\nAny removed legacy entry points\n\nAdd an architecture note explaining that alternate visual experiments must be implemented as theme options or feature branches, not permanently launched as separate application versions on arbitrary ports.\n\n25. Migration Strategy\n\nPerform the work in this order.\n\nStep 1: Repository and Runtime Audit\nIdentify all current application entry points\nIdentify which code runs on 3000, 3101, and 3106\nDetermine which implementation contains the newest features\nDocument duplicated and divergent code\nIdentify the canonical app\nStep 2: Consolidate Feature Code\nBring the restored book and newest Vision Waypoint functionality into the canonical app\nEliminate reliance on alternate frontend servers\nPreserve working functionality before deleting legacy code\nStep 3: Implement Shared Experience Shell\nShared header\nShared theme context\nShared top tabs\nShared connection status\nShared routed content area\nStep 4: Convert Side Overlays to Full Routes\nChapters\nMap\nArtifacts\nMessages\nStep 5: Implement Semantic Theme System\nVerdant Depths\nMoonlit Blue\nApplication preference\nExperience override\nToken migration\nStep 6: Standardize Captain Navigation\nGlobal shell\nCaptain workspace shell\nBreadcrumbs\nPredictable parent navigation\nActive states\nStep 7: Implement Animation Lifecycle System\nRoute transitions\nComponent transitions\nState-change animation\nAmbient animation\nReduced motion\nCleanup and replay correctness\nStep 8: Port Consolidation\nEnforce port 3000\nRemove obsolete scripts and references\nUpdate tests and documentation\nConfirm no hidden alternate app remains\nStep 9: Verification\nAutomated tests\nManual route pass\nAnimation replay pass\nTheme pass\nResponsive pass\nAccessibility pass\nRuntime and port pass\n\nDo not delete an alternate implementation until its unique functionality has been identified and either merged or intentionally rejected.\n\n26. Acceptance Criteria\n\nThe work is complete only when all of the following are true:\n\nThe interactive book remains available.\nChapters, Map, Artifacts, and Messages appear as prominent top-level experience tabs.\nEach section has a full routed content page.\nThe current right-side overlay tabs are removed as primary navigation.\nThe default application color scheme is the green Verdant Depths theme.\nEvery Vision Waypoint screen uses the same semantic theme system.\nMoonlit Blue can be selected through application settings.\nA Tall Tale can override the application theme.\nCaptain navigation uses one documented hierarchy.\nBrowser back and forward work predictably.\nEvery primary page has an intentional transition.\nRoute entry animations replay after returning to a page.\nRefreshing a page does not permanently suppress animation.\nModals and secondary interactions animate every time they open.\nReduced-motion behavior works.\nThe canonical application runs on port 3000.\nPorts 3101 and 3106 are no longer required or presented as separate versions.\nThe app does not silently switch to another port.\nAutomated tests pass.\nNo major content is obscured by reconnecting or historical-status overlays.\nDesktop, tablet, and mobile layouts remain usable.\nNo existing session, book, map, artifact, or message functionality is silently discarded.\nDocumentation reflects the consolidated architecture.\n27. Required Final Report\n\nAt completion, provide a detailed report containing:\n\nArchitecture Changes\nCanonical application entry point\nShared shell structure\nRoute structure\nTheme architecture\nNavigation hierarchy\nAnimation architecture\nFiles Changed\n\nGroup by:\n\nRouting\nComponents\nThemes\nNavigation\nAnimation\nRuntime configuration\nTests\nDocumentation\nRemoved legacy code\nLegacy Consolidation\n\nExplain:\n\nWhat was running on 3000\nWhat was running on 3101\nWhat was running on 3106\nWhich features differed\nHow the features were merged\nWhich obsolete entry points were removed\nTesting Results\n\nInclude:\n\nUnit tests\nIntegration tests\nEnd-to-end tests\nManual animation replay results\nTheme verification\nResponsive verification\nAccessibility verification\nPort verification\nRemaining Risks\n\nList any unresolved issues honestly.\n\nDo not claim completion if:\n\nSome pages still lack animation\nSome blue hardcoded screens remain\nAlternate ports are still needed\nSide overlays remain the only route to important content\nRefreshing or revisiting suppresses animation\nCaptain navigation still changes structure between screens\n28. Implementation Principle\n\nTreat this work as consolidation of the actual product, not creation of another prototype.\n\nThere must be:\n\nOne application\nOne canonical runtime\nOne navigation language\nOne theme system\nOne motion system\nOne coherent experience\n\nThe product may contain many voyages, visual themes, captain tools, player states, and story formats.\n\nIt must not contain several competing definitions of what the application itself is.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

[{ label: titleCase(parts[3]) }] : []),\r\n      ];\r\n  }\r\n  if (parts[0] === \"studio\" && parts[1] === \"vision-waypoints\")\r\n    return [\r\n      { href: \"/studio/library\", label: \"Library\" },\r\n      { href: \"/studio/vision-waypoints\", label: \"Vision Waypoints\" },\r\n      ...(parts[2] ? [{ label: \"Waypoint\" }] : []),\r\n    ];\r\n  if (parts[0] === \"captain\" && parts[1] === \"sessions\" && parts[2])\r\n    return [\r\n      { href: \"/captain/library\", label: \"Library\" },\r\n      { href: \"/captain/sessions\", label: \"Active Sessions\" },\r\n      { label: \"Session\" },\r\n    ];\r\n  if (parts[0] === \"captain\" && parts[1] === \"tales\" && parts[2])\r\n    return [{ href: \"/captain/library\", label: \"Library\" }, { label: \"Tall Tale\" }];\r\n  if (parts[0] === \"captain\" && parts[1] === \"voyages\" && parts[2])\r\n    return [\r\n      { href: \"/captain/library\", label: \"Library\" },\r\n      { label: parts[3] === \"player-preview\" ? \"Player Preview\" : \"Voyage\" },\r\n    ];\r\n  return [];\r\n}\r\n\r\nfunction titleCase(value: string) {\r\n  return value.replaceAll(\"-\", \" \").replace(/\\b\\w/g, (character) => character.toLocaleUpperCase());\r\n}\r\n\r\nfunction routeFamily(pathname: string) {\r\n  if (pathname.includes(\"settings\")) return \"settings\";\r\n  if (pathname.includes(\"library\") || pathname === \"/tales\" || pathname.endsWith(\"/history\")) return \"library\";\r\n  if (pathname.startsWith(\"/studio\")) return \"studio\";\r\n  if (pathname.startsWith(\"/captain\")) return \"captain\";\r\n  return \"page\";\r\n}\r\n\r\nfunction shellRouteMotion(pathname: string, mode: \"full\" | \"gentle\" | \"reduced\") {\r\n  if (mode === \"reduced\")\r\n    return {\r\n      initial: { opacity: 0 },\r\n      enter: { opacity: 1 },\r\n      exit: { opacity: 0 },\r\n      transition: { duration: 0.08 },\r\n    };\r\n  const family = routeFamily(pathname);\r\n  const duration = mode === \"gentle\" ? 0.28 : 0.48;\r\n  const variants =\r\n    family === \"library\"\r\n      ? { initial: { opacity: 0, y: 18 }, enter: { opacity: 1, y: 0 }, exit: { opacity: 0, y: -8 } }\r\n      : family === \"studio\"\r\n        ? { initial: { opacity: 0, scale: 0.99 }, enter: { opacity: 1, scale: 1 }, exit: { opacity: 0 } }\r\n        : family === \"captain\"\r\n          ? { initial: { opacity: 0, x: 20 }, enter: { opacity: 1, x: 0 }, exit: { opacity: 0, x: -12 } }\r\n          : family === \"settings\"\r\n            ? { initial: { opacity: 0, y: 12 }, enter: { opacity: 1, y: 0 }, exit: { opacity: 0 } }\r\n            : { initial: { opacity: 0 }, enter: { opacity: 1 }, exit: { opacity: 0 } };\r\n  return { ...variants, transition: { duration, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] as const } };\r\n}\r\nSTUDIO EDITOR NAVIGATION\r\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:120:  activeSessions: number;\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:195:  const [collapsedChapters, setCollapsedChapters] = useState<string[]>([]);\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:311:        .flatMap((chapter) => chapter.blocks.map((block) => ({ chapter, block })))\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:333:    const contexts = new Map<string, Set<string>>();\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:426:  const filteredArtifacts = useMemo(\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:513:      const block = next.chapters.flatMap((chapter) => chapter.blocks).find((candidate) => candidate.id === selectedId);\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:769:  const nav = [\"story\", \"settings\", \"assets\", \"locations\", \"artifacts\", \"versions\"] as const;\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:818:      <nav className=\"editor-section-nav\" aria-label=\"Tall Tale authoring sections\">\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:819:        {nav.map((item) => (\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:828:      </nav>\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:858:                <h2>Story pieces</h2>\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:859:                <p>Build, navigate, and inspect the complete story flow.</p>\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:861:              <div className=\"library-tabs\" role=\"tablist\" aria-label=\"Story tools\">\nsrc/components/studio/TaleEditor.tsx:870:                    {tab === \"blocks\" ? The launcher refuses an occupied port unless its state record, runtime root, PID, and listener ownership all match. Isolated validation may override its temporary test port, but no product route, package script, or fallback treats that as another application version. Alternate visual experiments belong in semantic themes or feature branches, not permanent servers on arbitrary ports.\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:3:`src/app/layout.tsx` is the canonical frontend entry point. All Player, Captain, Creator, Vision, settings, and Experience routes are part of this Next.js application; alternate themes are token sets, not separate frontends. The canonical local origin is `http://127.0.0.1:3000`.\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:19:The recorded-state fast path also verifies runtime root, canonical port, and socket ownership. A stale PID or a listener from another checkout therefore produces a diagnostic instead of a false “already running” result. Visual experiments must be developed as semantic themes or feature branches and merged back into this entry point; ports 3101 and 3106 are deprecated historical worktree choices, not supported runtimes.\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:23:- Unified role gateway: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:24:- Player URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tale/development-forever-treasure`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:25:- Player identity/library: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/player`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:28:- GM URL: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/quartermaster`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:30:- Animation showcase: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/dev/animations` (development only)\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:31:- Tall Tale catalog: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tales`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:32:- Creator Studio: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/studio/library`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:33:- Captain command/library: `http://127.0.0.1:3000/captain/library`\n.\\docs\\local-development.md:74:- **Port 3000 is occupied:** identify and stop the verified owning application, then rerun the launcher. [{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.2 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.2 seconds\nOutput:\n# Responsive behavior\n\n## Product shell and non-immersive workspaces\n\nPublic discovery and Player, Captain, and Creator workspaces use a capped full-width shell. Desktop keeps the product mark and route-aware navigation in one sticky row. At compact widths, a named Menu control opens an overlaid navigation panel, moves focus to its first link, closes on Escape or backdrop activation, and returns focus to the trigger. The page remains available to assistive technology in document order, and the skip link targets the shared main-content wrapper.\n\nPublic catalog filters collapse from four columns to one readable stack. Player library controls disappear when the library is genuinely empty. Captain section tabs use two balanced rows at phone widths rather than a horizontally scrolling strip; voyage and Studio cards expand at wide widths instead of leaving empty grid tracks. All controls retain a minimum touch height, shell padding includes safe-area insets, and mobile product chrome never overlays the landing role actions.\n\n## Complete shell\n\nDesktop uses the available monitor as a layered story workspace with persistent one-action navigation and compact objective access. Mobile switches to a single content column, labeled bottom navigation, safe-area padding, separate section views, and a compact return-to-clue control. The map always has a text alternative. Artifact inspection becomes a full-screen sheet on narrow screens. Fullscreen mode removes secondary chrome without removing navigation or the objective.\n\nAbove 1100px, chart, open journal, and relic display form a three-region physical workspace. At 1100px and below, bottom navigation exposes one view at a time. At 650px, the journal becomes one vertically readable parchment sheet with safe-area-aware bottom controls. Short phone landscape hides the spine/left sheet and uses a compact single-page composition. Capability choices use CSS width, height, orientation, hover-independent controls, safe-area insets, and `prefers-reduced-motion`; no user-agent sniffing is used.\n\nThe published Tall Tale journal follows the same physical rules while keeping supporting controls secondary. Desktop preserves the dominant open book and contextual side drawers. Compact widths reduce header and tray density while retaining two-page structure. Phone portrait and short landscape use one readable sheet, full-width bottom drawers for chapters/maps/artifacts/messages, touch-safe actions, and a persistent Return to Current Objective control when the reader is behind live progress. Text scaling changes ink size without transforming the entire interface, and reduced motion replaces ceremonial motion with direct, semantically equivalent state changes.\n\nValidated viewports: 2560Ã—1440, 1920Ã—1080, 1440Ã—900, 390Ã—844, 430Ã—932, and 844Ã—390. Product-shell, catalog, Captain, and Studio bounds were also inspected at their representative breakpoints with no document-level horizontal overflow.\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

Desktop preserves the dominant open book and contextual side drawers. Compact widths reduce header and tray density while retaining two-page structure. Phone portrait and short landscape use one readable sheet, full-width bottom drawers for chapters/maps/artifacts/messages, touch-safe actions, and a persistent Return to Current Objective control when the reader is behind live progress. Text scaling changes ink size without transforming the entire interface, and reduced motion replaces ceremonial motion with direct, semantically equivalent state changes.\n+The routed Tall Tale Experience keeps its four section destinations visible as a horizontal parchment-and-brass tablist. Desktop and tablet show Chapters, Map, Artifacts, and Messages above a full-width routed content region; compact widths keep the same destinations in a touch-safe horizontally scrollable row. No essential section returns to a side drawer or covers the current content. The shell, connection, theme, audio, motion, and reading providers remain mounted while only the keyed tabpanel changes.\n \n-Validated viewports: 2560×1440, 1920×1080, 1440×900, 390×844, 430×932, and 844×390. Product-shell, catalog, Captain, and Studio bounds were also inspected at their representative breakpoints with no document-level horizontal overflow.\n+Chapters preserves the physical two-page book where space permits and becomes a readable single-page composition on phones and short landscape screens. Map receives its own chart viewport and stacks selected-location detail below it when narrow. Artifacts become a one-column gallery plus inspection flow, and Messages become a readable list/detail stack. The compact connection notice remains in document flow. Text scaling changes ink size without transforming the entire interface, and reduced motion replaces spatial ceremonies with fast, semantically equivalent opacity/state changes.\n+\n+The consolidation responsive pass exercises 375, 430, 768, 1024, 1440, and 1920 pixel widths. The product shell switches between mobile and desktop navigation without horizontal overflow; Experience tabs remain reachable, and route content never becomes a narrow side overlay. Existing physical-workspace checks also retain short-landscape and safe-area coverage.\n*** Update File: C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation\\Development_Docs\\Unified_Experience_Shell_Consolidation_Implementation_Record.md\n@@\n - `docs/journal-system.md`\n - `docs/local-development.md`\n+- `docs/responsive-behavior.md`\n - `docs/testing.md`\n - this implementation record\n*** End Patch";

It is not required by this branch, but its owner must stop it when that task is finished.\ndocs\\asset-licenses.md:9:- `public/animations/lottie/moonlit-waves.json`\ndocs\\architecture.md:40:The Player Library is the place where Tall Tales are browsed. The immersive Experience shell is the place where Tall Tales are played. Durable Player routes use `/player/playthroughs/[id]/journal/{chapters,map,artifacts,messages}`; token-compatible active routes use `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/{chapters,map,artifacts,messages}`. Legacy base and archive URLs redirect to `chapters` rather than maintaining another renderer.\ndocs\\architecture.md:42:`TallTaleJournalSession` is a persistent route layout: identity, theme context, header, section tabs, SSE reconciliation, audio/motion state, and current objective remain mounted while the keyed content region changes. Chapters retains `PhysicalJournalBook`; Map, Artifacts, and Messages are full Player-safe pages that consume the same projection. Active and completed modes share these components, with completed mode disabling progression mutations. Reading position, map zoom/selection, artifact selection, and message read state persist separately from progression through `PlayerProfile.preferences`.\ndocs\\architecture.md:50:`src/styles/tokens.css` is the authoritative semantic theme layer. The root defaults to **Verdant Depths** and `[data-theme=\"moonlit-blue\"]` replaces the semantic values for **Moonlit Blue**. Player and staff application preferences are persisted in their existing JSON preference records and cached locally only as a fallback. A Tall Tale stores `APPLICATION`, `VERDANT_DEPTHS`, or `MOONLIT_BLUE`; an explicit Tale value is scoped to the Experience root and therefore overrides, but never overwrites, the application preference.\ndocs\\design-system.md:15:All application chrome and operational surfaces consume the `--color-*` aliases in `tokens.css`. **Verdant Depths** is the default deep green-black, muted emerald, warm-brass, cream-parchment scheme. **Moonlit Blue** replaces water, fog, glow, surfaces, borders, text, focus, and navigation values with a controlled navy/cyan family. Vision Waypoint and Companion screens use those same aliases and are not independent blue applications.\ndocs\\design-system.md:17:Theme precedence is: explicit Experience override, persisted Player or staff application preference, then Verdant Depths. `APPLICATION` on a Tall Tale means inherit. Stable parchment pigments may remain material-specific, but surrounding atmosphere, focus, metal, shadow, status, form, and navigation colors must use semantic tokens. New visual experiments extend this theme layer; they must not introduce a separate root palette or alternate frontend runtime.\ndocs\\design-system.md:25:Tokens live in `src/styles/tokens.css`: abyss navy `#061316`, ocean green `#123b3a`, parchment `#d9c69a`, antique brass `#b89551`, crimson `#782f35`, and moonlit teal `#79d2c3`. Georgia/system serif keeps story text readable; Arial is reserved for small operational labels. Paper grain, edge wear, chart lines, brass frames, restrained glow, and vignette are original CSS/SVG materials. Focus is a 3px warm-gold outline. Layers reserve 20–35 for persistent chrome, 60 for journal opening, 70 for ceremony controls, and 90 for GM confirmations.\ndocs\\local-development.md:19:The recorded-state fast path also verifies runtime root, canonical port, and socket ownership. A stale PID or a listener from another checkout therefore produces a diagnostic instead of a false “already running” result. Visual experiments must be developed as semantic themes or feature branches and merged back into this entry point; ports 3101 and 3106 are deprecated historical worktree choices, not supported runtimes.\ndocs\\local-development.md:74:- **Port 3000 is occupied:** identify and stop the verified owning application, then rerun the launcher. Startup fails clearly and never selects port 3001, 3101, 3106, or another fallback; it also never kills an unknown listener.\ndocs\\journal-system.md:7:Published playthroughs use one persistent Experience renderer at `/player/playthroughs/[playthroughId]/journal/{chapters,map,artifacts,messages}`. Token-compatible active routes render the same component at `/play/[taleSlug]/session/[sessionId]/{chapters,map,artifacts,messages}`, and old base/archive routes redirect to Chapters. Active, paused, resumed, and completed playthroughs therefore share one visual and interaction model. The original campaign journal and the published Tall Tale journal also share `PhysicalJournalBook` and `PageFlipBook`, preserving the cover, attached latch, seal, binding, left/right page geometry, page stacks, page curl, reduced-motion reader, and responsive transformations.\ndocs\\journal-system.md:15:Chapters keeps the book dominant. Map uses a full chart, released markers, legend, zoom, and selected-location detail. Artifacts uses a scalable gallery plus inspection panel. Messages uses a history column and readable letter panel with persistent read state. The old right-side section buttons and essential drawers are removed. The horizontal parchment/brass tab row scrolls at narrow widths, remains separate from the objective/status region, supports Arrow/Home/End keyboard navigation, and drives real URLs. Page-turn controls, actions, text scaling, live announcements, focus-visible states, route focus transfer, and reduced-motion behavior remain keyboard and assistive-technology accessible.\ndocs\\codex-handoff.md:35:The landing, access gate, player journal workspace, chart, treasure altar, quest ledger, ship's log, finale chamber, and quartermaster dashboard share the physical moonlit-journal design system. ## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T16:59:11.069Z

The diff audit also exposed Prisma alignment churn from generation: the actual schema change is one `preferences` field per schema, but automatic formatting had realigned unrelated Vision models. I’m removing that normalization noise so the commit preserves authored formatting and contains only the intended schema additions.

## Tool Call: exec
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T16:59:14.702Z

const cwd="C:\\Users\\kkids\\Documents\\Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation";
for (const rel of ["prisma/schema.prisma","prisma/schema.sqlite.prisma"]) {
  const d=await tools.shell_command({command:`git diff -R --no-color --unified=1 -- "${rel}"`,workdir:cwd,timeout_ms:10000});
  const lines=String(d).split(/\r?\n/);
  const first=lines.findIndex(l=>l.startsWith("@@"));
  if(first<0) throw new Error("No reverse diff for "+rel);
  const patch="*** Begin Patch\n*** Update File: "+cwd+"\\"+rel.replaceAll("/","\\")+"\n"+lines.slice(first).join("\n")+"\n*** End Patch";
  await tools.apply_patch(patch);
}
text("Reverted schema formatting churn");

const rel="prisma/schema.prisma";
const d=await tools.shell_command({command:`git diff -R --no-color --unified=1 -- "${rel}"`,workdir:cwd,timeout_ms:10000});
const lines=String(d).split(/\r?\n/);

## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T17:04:01.659Z

Implemented, validated, committed, synchronized, and pushed on `codex/unified-experience-shell`. The branch is clean and exactly matches remote SHA `826701e6a34951706576b80e05771b5b961b9c3c`.

The complete report is in [Unified Experience Shell Consolidation Implementation Record](</C:/Users/kkids/Documents/Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation/Development_Docs/Unified_Experience_Shell_Consolidation_Implementation_Record.md>).

## Architecture changes

- One canonical Next.js application root at `src/app/layout.tsx`.
- Canonical startup is `npm run dev:full` on `127.0.0.1:3000`.
- Active and completed voyages share one persistent Experience shell.
- Chapters, Map, Artifacts, and Messages are full routed pages with real URLs, deep links, browser history, active tab state, and route-keyed transitions.
- The restored physical book remains the Chapters presentation, including page turns, editions, endpapers, numbering, responsive behavior, and completed read-only mode.
- Map, Artifacts, and Messages now have full-page responsive layouts with persisted selection/zoom/read state.
- Large side-overlay navigation was removed as the primary mechanism.
- Compact, truthful connection status now sits in the header/document flow.
- Verdant Depths is the semantic default; Moonlit Blue is selectable.
- Player and staff preferences persist in existing profile preference records, with browser storage limited to anonymous/offline fallback.
- Tall Tales support application inheritance, Verdant override, or Moonlit override.
- Product navigation is role-aware, with stable Library, History/Sessions, Studio, and Settings destinations plus nested breadcrumbs.
- Global providers and connections stay mounted while only keyed route content transitions.

Key implementations include [TallTaleJournalSession.tsx](</C:/Users/kkids/Documents/Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation/src/components/player/journal/TallTaleJournalSession.tsx>), [ExperienceSectionPages.tsx](</C:/Users/kkids/Documents/Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation/src/components/player/journal/ExperienceSectionPages.tsx>), [ProductShell.tsx](</C:/Users/kkids/Documents/Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation/src/components/shell/ProductShell.tsx>), and [tokens.css](</C:/Users/kkids/Documents/Codex_TreasureHunt_Consolidation/src/styles/tokens.css>).

## Legacy consolidation

- Port 3000: canonical main-checkout Next.js application, still running under the concurrent task.
- Port 3101: verified Phase B2 worktree server. Its cumulative B1–B6 functionality was merged from the Phase B6 descendant; its externally owned process was not stopped.
- Port 3106: no listener and no required implementation.
- Removed canonical reliance on alternate ports, fallback-port startup, duplicated active/completed journal renderers, right-side essential drawers, and independent hardcoded Vision blue surfaces.
- A strict conflict test confirmed port 3000 produces `EADDRINUSE`, exits 1, preserves the existing owner, and does not fall back to 3001, 3101, or 3106.
